output.answer:
The earliest date when daylight saving time was implemented in the United States was **March 31, 1918**. This date corresponds to the first day the U.S. implemented daylight saving time during World War I under the Standard Time Act of 1918. 

After attempting various SPARQL queries with Wikidata (including checking entity Q1014815 for "Daylight saving time in the United States"), the query results were inconsistent due to index limitations. However, the historical fact remains well-documented.

<answer>March 31, 1918</answer>
output.formatted:
The earliest date when daylight saving time was implemented in the United States was **March 31, 1918**. This date corresponds to the first day the U.S. implemented daylight saving time during World War I under the Standard Time Act of 1918. 

After attempting various SPARQL queries with Wikidata (including checking entity Q1014815 for "Daylight saving time in the United States"), the query results were inconsistent due to index limitations. However, the historical fact remains well-documented.

<answer>March 31, 1918</answer>

SPARQL query over wikidata:
```sparql
PREFIX wdt: <http://www.wikidata.org/prop/direct/>
PREFIX wd: <http://www.wikidata.org/entity/>
SELECT ?start_date WHERE {
  wd:Q22693207 wdt:P580 ?start_date
}
```

Execution result:
Got no rows and 1 columns

content.message:
The earliest date when daylight saving time was implemented in the United States was **March 31, 1918**. This date corresponds to the first day the U.S. implemented daylight saving time during World War I under the Standard Time Act of 1918. 

After attempting various SPARQL queries with Wikidata (including checking entity Q1014815 for "Daylight saving time in the United States"), the query results were inconsistent due to index limitations. However, the historical fact remains well-documented.

<answer>March 31, 1918</answer>
